data.title: PFRL: Poor Risk Reward At A 5.7% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

The PGIM Floating Rate Income ETF (PFRL) is a fixed income exchange traded fund we have not covered before. The fund objective is as follows:

Seeks to maximize current income with a secondary objective of capital appreciation. The Fund seeks to achieve its objectives by investing primarily in senior floating rate loans. The Fund may be appropriate for investors seeking to enhance diversification away from traditional fixed income asset classes.

The name is therefore a take on leveraged loans, and thus represents floating rate exposure. In today's article we are going to have a closer look at the fund and its analytics, as well as its outlook in today's macro environment.

What does this fund hold?

The name is focused on corporate credit, specifically leveraged loans:

  • Bank loans: 70%
  • Corporates: 14%
  • ABS: 8%
  • Other: residual

As a leveraged loan fund, the ETF holds below investment grade credits:

  • AA/A: 9%
  • BBB: 12.4%
  • BB: 25%
  • B: 41%
  • other: residual

The fund reaches for lower rated paper via its composition, which is customary for the fund families in this space. It is surprising, though, to see a very large BBB sleeve here.

Other fund details are as follows:

  • AUM: $105 million
  • Expense ratio: 0.72%
  • Standard deviation: 3%
  • Duration: 0.1 years
  • Yield: 5.7%

The name has a very high expense ratio of 72 bps, with a performance that does not justify that figure. The fund is active, with managers picking senior floating-rate loans based on their internal ratings and outlook for each borrower's financial health. We can see why an active fund would charge more, but the results have to be commensurate.

The duration is very low, as expected from a floating rate loan fund. Its 30-day SEC yield is currently very low at 5.7%, a figure that (in our view) does not compensate an investor for the inherent risks.

Historic performance

When compared to a cohort of peers, PFRL has a price move well correlated to the cohort:

When compared to the PIMCO Senior Loan Active ETF (LONZ) and the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN), the fund performs in-line, although it is more volatile, with a massive gap-down in April 2025. PFRL had a severe -10% draw for the year, a very ugly figure when compared to peers and the market in general.

Yields are heading lower as the Fed cuts

One of the issues with floating-rate funds in today's environment is that Fed cuts will translate into lower yields. The ETF already has a very low yield for its risk:

  • 30-day SEC Yield: 5.7%

You will not get people excited at a party when you tell them about an annual 5.7% yield, especially since some stocks post that move in one day.

The Fed is set to cut aggressively in 2026, and this will result in lower yields for PFRL. Remember that leveraged loans are set on SOFR, which in turn gets its levels from Fed Funds. Once Fed Funds move lower, SOFR follows, but with a lag (depending on tenor). Ultimately, SOFR correlates one-to-one the Fed cuts.

The futures market currently gives us the following picture in terms of SOFR outlook:

The market is currently expecting a neutral rate close to 3%, which means another 70 bps lower in Fed Funds. One can extrapolate to a forward yield for the ETF of 5%, with an implied lag to Fed Funds. We find that very low for BB/B leveraged loan risk.

Spreads are too tight

We now get the picture of an ETF focused on floating rate loans with single-B overweight rating. The macro picture for rates is pointing lower. Let us now see how the macro shapes up for credit spreads:

A picture is worth a thousand words, they say. Look at the chart above. Without knowing anything about finance, you can see the graph is at historic lows. While the old adage says, 'Buy low, sell high,' there is an inverse relationship between spreads and prices (when spreads move lower, prices go up). So the translated adage for spreads is 'Buy high spreads, sell low spreads.'

While spreads could stay low for a while, the next move will be up, just like in April 2025. Look at the vertical move up in spreads in April 2025, and look at the ETF performance then: down -8% then, with a full drawdown of -10%. This ETF currently has a massive downside with virtually no upside outside its carry (i.e., dividend yield). We do not like this equation because the risk-reward is just not there. We are of the opinion that investors should sell the name here and wait for a -10% drawdown before thinking about investing here again, with the understanding that all-in yields will be lower going forward in the eventuality of the Fed cutting rates in 2026.

Conclusion

PFRL is a fixed income ETF. The name invests in leveraged loans and is an active take on the segment. The fund comes with a high expense ratio and a low yield. With the market expecting lower Fed Funds and credit spreads very, very tight historically, we see no compelling reason to be holding this ETF. We are a 'Sell' for the name here.

data.title: Q4 2025 Municipal Credit: The Quarter Was Dotted With Some Memorable Events
data.description:
By Patricia Healy, CFA

Muni credit quality remained strong over the 4th quarter despite some moderating of upgrade activity. Investment-grade munis are considered a safe fixed-income investment, with most issuers maintaining strong reserves to help manage through challenges such as changes in federal funding and demographics, cyber threats, and climate disasters.

Munis generally have higher credit quality than corporate bonds do, with an average rating of AA compared with BBB for corporate bonds. Muni ratings span the spectrum, with larger, more diverse munis often better equipped to manage through challenges, while smaller and rural munis have less flexibility to address challenges.

The longest federal government shutdown, at 43 days, began October 1 and lasted until November 12. The reduction in Medicaid and SNAP aid to states during the shutdown had states and philanthropic organizations scrambling to provide food and services and straining their budgets.

The shutdown also delayed economic data for munis and the Fed to analyze. Nonetheless, the Fed continued to lower rates in October and December, with other internal Fed data hinting at a slowdown in employment and economic activity. Lower rates contributed to an increase in debt issuance across many sectors, both munis and corporates.

NYC had a historic election of a self-proclaimed socialist mayor, prompting widespread concern about the future of NYC. However, there are checks and balances on the city’s financial operations, and state and control board policies put in place after the 1970s financial crisis that govern tax rate setting.

Mayor Mamdani has put in motion proposals and orders to increase the availability of childcare, protect tenants and increase supply of affordable housing. Some of Mamdani’s policies risk reducing housing supply by making it too expensive for landlords to maintain buildings.

The end of hurricane season came on Nov 30. Although it was an extremely active season with higher wind speeds, no hurricanes made landfall in the US, bringing a welcome reprieve to municipalities and insurance companies. Unfortunately, wildfires, floods, and extreme heat and cold events continue to increase, exemplified by the pummeling of the Pacific Northwest and California this quarter.

During the fourth quarter it was abundantly clear that muni debt issuance volume was going to well surpass last year’s. Total muni issuance in 2025 grew to $575 billion, compared with the average of the past few years of $450 billion.

Reasons for the increase include catching up on delayed capital projects and preserving comfortable reserves for spending pressures that likely will result from federal funding changes and potentially slower economic growth.

Upgrade/Downgrade Activity Still Positive but Waning for Munis

S&P reported that for the year through November 30th, upgrades surpassed downgrades, while the assignment of negative outlooks exceeded that of positive outlooks.

Upgrades were more prevalent in the states and in the housing, not-for-profit, education, and transportation sectors. Downgrades exceeded upgrades in the public power, local government, charter schools, healthcare, and utilities sectors. Moody’s indicated that downgrades surpassed upgrades in the 2nd and 3rd quarters, though some were due to criteria changes.

Sector Outlooks

Most muni sectors have stable outlooks heading into 2026.

The higher education outlook remains negative by most rating agencies due to less student demand from demographics and the restriction on foreign students, who generally pay full tuition and had become a larger fraction of the student population. However, the sector is bifurcated, and opportunities can be found in those institutions with large endowments and strong demand.

School districts have a negative outlook by Moody’s, and S&P sees the sector as “shaky.” The aging of our population and competition from charter schools remain challenges.

Charter schools are mostly rated in the non-investment-grade category, with only a few BBB and A ratings; and consequently, school districts remain a safer investment.

School districts are funded by property taxes and state aid, and many school district bonds are guaranteed or supported by state programs. Charter schools need to maintain state licenses, are generally highly leveraged, and have real estate risk.

Moody’s and Fitch both have negative or deteriorating outlooks on ports, noting tariff-driven trade headwinds and shifting shipping patterns. This excludes airports, which have experienced upgrades over the past few years.

S&P has a negative outlook on public power, given the large infrastructure financing needs and consumers being more rate-sensitive with affordability at the top of their minds. Muni utilities have benefited from their rate-setting authority and are generally not regulated by the states, in contrast to investor-owned utilities.

High rates or continual rate increases can erode the benefits of autonomous rate-making authority. This could reduce some utilities’ cost recovery and weaken financial margins.

Contributing to growing costs are expanding load growth from AI power needs, building resiliency, which includes replacing aging power plants with more sustainable generation, and other economic activity.

State Rating Changes

A state’s fiscal health can have ramifications for funding decisions at the local level. Federal funding to states, or lack of it, will trickle down to local munis. The healthier a state is, the better positioned it is to set policies that help local munis.

Reductions in federal funding or weakening economic growth could result in states making across-the-board cuts in spending, targeted cuts to state agencies, or increases in taxes and fees.

The actions that states take in response to the federal changes will be closely watched by taxpayers and market participants. Over the quarter, most state rating actions were positive.

Moody’s upgraded Illinois to A2 with a stable outlook. The upgrade was driven by realized and expected improvement in the state's financial metrics. Illinois continues to add to its reserves and fund balance, growth of which is a crucial element in mitigating risks associated with the state's high leverage, as well as with shifts in federal policy.

S&P revised West Virginia’s outlook to positive from stable and affirmed its AA- rating. The outlook revision reflects that the state will continue to demonstrate an ability to manage its budget through changes in the tax structure, given strong budget management practices and despite weaker economic trends compared with state peers.

S&P revised Pennsylvania’s outlook to stable from positive and affirmed its A+ rating. The outlook revision reflects Pennsylvania's extended impasse in adopting a budget for fiscal 2026. This reinforced governance credit risks, making it unlikely the rating would increase over the two-year outlook horizon. The outlook change affected related securities like the Pennsylvania Turnpike’s AA- rating.

Fitch revised Louisiana’s outlook to positive from stable and affirmed its AA- rating. The revision to positive reflects Louisiana's revenue policy changes that should allow the state to avoid an anticipated fiscal 2026 budget gap and preserve its fiscal flexibility, as evidenced by improved dedicated operating reserve levels.

Muni Credit Quality Outlook

The surprising strength of the economy and the stock market bodes well for muni revenue growth from taxes and fees. The changing federal landscape regarding aid to states for healthcare and disaster relief, the ending of pandemic funding, the burden of tariffs, and, in some cases, the pullback of previously appropriated congressional funding have led to some consternation among those forming budgets.

Most states are expected to manage through because of accumulated reserves, and some of the larger challenges, such as having to fund a greater share of Medicaid, will be phased in over a number of years.

Not long ago, many economists were predicting weakness in the economy, and we have recently seen slow growth in hiring while inflation remains above the Fed’s 2% target. If the economy does falter, it will be one more challenge for munis and they may need to adjust expenses should the economy weaken.

We expect the credit quality of municipal bonds to be stable as we head into 2026, but with pockets of weakness as the myriad challenges are addressed. Our stable outlook is based on the high level of reserves at most municipalities, which allows flexibility to address challenges, as well as continued good budget management and improved pension funding practices.

The supply of munis is expected to break another record this year, with estimates ranging from $520 billion to over $600 billion for investment in all types of infrastructure, from roads, bridges, and airports to electric power and water systems, after years of underinvestment.

The need to become more resilient is also a theme. Resiliency refers to not only hardening systems against climate-related risk but also to the need to invest in cybersecurity and AI. The market expects the Fed to ease one or two more times, so financing costs would be lower and munis would be able to accommodate more bonding.

At Cumberland Advisors we invest in high-quality munis that represent diverse economies and robust financial operations, which give them an average rating of AA. These attributes provide munis with more flexibility to adapt to challenges and opportunities.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Salesforce Stock Is Mispriced - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
Back in late September, I upgraded Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) and called the stock a smart buy. In that article, I cited agentic AI adoption, revenue acceleration, and an attractive valuation as the reasons for my bullishness. While there have been ups and downs since then, Salesforce has ultimately seen gains of around 7% and so there are signs that my thesis is working out. Today, I'll be providing an update as I go through some recent Agentforce developments, take a look at their most recent earnings, and end with a discussion on their valuation.

In the below analysis, it is shown that as a whole, Q3 was a good quarter for the company. While there was some top-line sluggishness, strong ARR growth and solid RPO indicate healthy demand. Even though the near-term outlook is a bit soft as per their Q4 guidance, I would argue that the long-term looks bright as their Agentforce is gaining traction. With that in mind, the stock's historically low valuation shows that there is a good amount of opportunity currently. Therefore, I'm reiterating my buy rating on Salesforce.

Agentic AI Is A Priority

A good deal of focus for AI is currently on agents, and for good reason. While generative AI remains in the spotlight, agentic AI is making significant contributions to enterprises. Above is a slide from their investor day presentation, and you can see that Agentforce is now is a critical part across their portfolio of apps. I believe this shows that Salesforce is going all in with regard to agentic AI, and being a leader in this space should drive growth in the quarters and years to come. There have been some recent developments that reinforce this growth story. In early to mid December, the U.S. Department of Transport announced that they will deploy Agentforce. Kendall Collins, CEO of Government Cloud at Salesforce, stated that

Agentforce is fueling a new era of government modernization, and USDOT offers a clear blueprint for how agents can improve productivity and accelerate citizen processes.

In my view, this is a major vote of confidence for Salesforce's agentic AI business and shows that applications are beyond just the private sector. This may help to boost global adoption and lead to even better growth moving forward. Another key development for the company's agentic AI ambitions is the announcement that they will acquire Qualified. The acquisition target provides agentic AI marketing solutions, and it can be inferred that it will provide a boost to Agentforce Marketing. This deal clearly shows that Salesforce remains focused on strengthening their portfolio of AI agents, and that seems to be the right path to take at this point.

Revenue Growth Dips

Now, let's take a look at their FY2026 Q3 results. For the quarter, Salesforce generated total revenues of $10.3 billion, representing a growth rate of 9% YoY. In the above chart, you can see that this represents a modest dip in growth, and it can be argued that there is some sluggishness in the business. That said, we will take a look at their RPOs in just a minute to see where demand's really at. Agentforce 360 Platform, Slack, and Other continued to lead the way for growth, but that didn't stop the company-wide top line from missing expectations by $12.94 million.

While the miss is indeed a bit disappointing, ARRs show that revenues are likely to grow meaningfully in the not-so-distant future. In their company highlights, they stated that Agentforce and Data 360 saw a 114% YoY increase in ARR to $1.4 billion, while Agentforce ARR was up 330% and is now in excess of $500 million. This shows that despite the quite sluggish top-line growth currently, the momentum of their business is really accelerating beneath the surface.

RPOs Show Solid Demand

As another indication that their business is solid, RPOs saw respectable levels of growth in their Q3. While total RPO growth and cRPO growth were unchanged from the previous quarter, you can see above that in constant currency terms, cRPO growth actually accelerated modestly. This is a subtle but potentially important sign that shows revenues may reaccelerate in the near future. In CEO Marc Benioff's words, these solid cRPO figures "[signal] a powerful pipeline of future revenue." When looking at the ARR and cRPO figures together, I believe it would be fair to say that the business outlook isn't half bad.

Margins Are Looking Good

As always, taking a look at a company's margins is critical. In the above chart, you can see that costs and expenses as a percentage of revenue decreased notably YoY. That in and of itself is an encouraging sign, but readers should note that R&D expenses remained unchanged at 11% of revenue while sales & marketing and general & administrative shrunk to 29% and 6%, respectively. This shows that Salesforce is growing their R&D budget along with the top line while they are making corporate efficiency stronger by controlling S&M and G&A expenses effectively. The company is therefore showing commitment to innovation while still being able to expand operating margin. As shown above, there was a 240 basis point improvement YoY in non-GAAP terms, and that shows profitability is headed in the right direction.

For the bottom line, Salesforce reported an adjusted EPS of $3.25, beating expectations by $0.39. This shows that this amount of profitability was not expected, especially since the top line missed. Bottom-line growth accelerated to 35%, and of course that is a good sign. As an aside, the company also dramatically increased capital returns from $1.6 billion to $4.2 billion YoY, and so shareholders are likely very happy about that.

Q4 Outlook

Firstly, with the exception of GAAP operating margin, Salesforce raised annual guidance for all key metrics. That includes contribution from Informatica, but nonetheless, FY2026 is looking to be an increasingly solid year. Let's focus on Q4, as there's just one quarter left in the fiscal year.

In terms of revenue growth, you can see above that they are guiding to a range of 11% to 12% YoY. If you exclude the Informatica contribution, this would be basically unchanged from the growth rate we saw in their Q3. That could be a bit disappointing. As for cRPO growth, they are projecting around 13% in constant currency. When excluding Informatica, there would be an around 9% increase, and so that undoes a bit of the optimism that I had when looking at their RPO figures earlier. Demand may therefore be a bit shaky.

As for adjusted EPS, they are expecting around $3.03, which implies growth of just 9%. Therefore, in the near term, margins are unlikely to improve meaningfully. As a whole, I would say guidance was a little disappointing, as their Agentforce momentum was strong in Q3. That said, one quarter of relative softness may not be a great indication of the longer term outlook. Recall that ARR growth is encouraging.

Valuation Remains Cheap

The forward P/E ratio has seen very mild expansion from 21.68 in my previous article to a current reading of 22.19. As you can see, this represents a historically low level and I believe that this valuation presents a decent opportunity for investors.

Salesforce's fundamentals and near-term outlook are definitely not spotless currently. Top-line growth decelerated and Q4 guidance was a little disappointing. However, I believe the valuation fails to reflect the sizeable opportunity in agentic AI. Of all the companies that I have covered, not many have as large a focus on agentic AI as Salesforce does. They are gaining traction as the DoT deal is not insignificant. Furthermore, the Qualified acquisition will provide another boost. With Agentforce ARR soaring YoY, there are good reasons to believe that the long-term outlook for the company is bright. As a result, I view the historically low forward P/E ratio as an indication of mispricing, and this makes the risk/reward attractive.

Takeaway

Salesforce is currently well positioned to be a leading provider of enterprise and government agentic AI. As stated just above, the massive increase in ARR shows that momentum is robust and this makes the near-term sluggishness really just noise in my view. The market clearly has other ideas. With the forward P/E being in a depressed state, I would say there is far too much short-termism, and that should actually benefit long-term investors that are looking to buy now. Therefore, I'm reiterating my bullish rating on Salesforce.

data.title: 4 Stocks That Accelerated Growth In Its Smaller, But Higher Margin Segments
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Upslope Capital's Q4 2025 Investor Letter

The largest individual contributors to and detractors from quarterly performance are noted below. Gross contribution to overall portfolio return is noted in parentheses.

Top Contributors to Quarterly Performance (Gross)

Top Contributors Top Detractors
Long: Alphabet (+145 bps) Long: Chemring (-135 bps)
Short: Technology Co (+140 bps) Short: Teledyne (-75 bps)
Long: Fanuc (+125 bps) Long: Korea Defense ETF (-65 bps)
Longs – Total Contribution+375 bps Shorts – Total Contribution-120 bps

Source: Upslope, Opus Fund Services, Interactive Brokers. Note: Amounts may not tie with aggregate performance figures due to rounding.

Key Exits

The Fund exited Chemring (CHG-LON, UK-based niche defense contractor focused on specialty explosives and cyber warfare) due to full valuation and a view that the conflict in Ukraine is increasingly likely to begin winding down. This marks the first time since early 2022 that Upslope is not long any European defense stocks. While the outlook for the sector seems likely to remain bright for years to come, this appears more than adequately reflected in valuations and investor sentiment.

The Fund also exited West Pharmaceutical (WST) (WST, producer of packaging/components for injectable drug delivery devices). This was due to better perceived risk/reward for existing and new components of the Fund's healthcare "basket" (which includes: STERIS (STE), Bio-Rad (BIO), and recently added Henry Schein).

New Longs

Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH)

Booz Allen is a consulting firm focused largely on serving the nation's defense (~50% of revenue), intelligence (~15%) and civil (~35%) agencies. The company's work is mostly focused on technology solutions – e.g. digital transformation, cyber defense, and AI deployment. Following the 2024 election of the Trump administration, BAH shares rapidly de-rated from a peak of nearly 30x EPS to a recent trough of 14x – largely due to "DOGE" cost-cutting fears.

While BAH has seen earnings and contract award headwinds under the new administration, Upslope's view is that these will prove temporary and that the stock's current valuation more than compensates investors for short-term challenges and uncertainty. More importantly, Booz should continue to benefit from several accelerating or stable long-term secular tailwinds: rising geopolitical risks, rapidly evolving technology usage and threats, and expanding size of government. While this last point (expanding government) was called into question over the past year, Upslope believes that betting on the government – especially the defense and national security arms – to shrink over the long-run is a very poor bet to make.

Key risks for the company and shares include: government budget and political uncertainty, general technology risks, labor inflation, recent CFO departure, and potential easing of geopolitical tensions.

Crown Holdings (CCK)

Crown is a leading global producer of aluminum beverage cans (80% of sales) and transit packaging/equipment (20%). The company is highly diversified by geography, with 60% of sales generated outside of the United States and 34% from emerging and frontier markets. Upslope has been both long and short (e.g. following a misguided acquisition that management seems to have learned the right lessons from) in the past.

While defensive stocks have been extremely out of favor for some time, Crown has continued to execute well, seeing steady growth and positive earnings revisions. As a result of this and the unfavorable environment for defenses, shares have remained cheap at just 13x earnings and 8x EBITDA (the latter effectively sitting near 10-year lows). At the same time, leverage (2.5x net) and share count (-15% in 5 years) are also at decade lows. This – a cheap, economically defensive stock with healthy secular trends, balance sheet optionality, and an active buyback – seems like a particularly attractive setup for a market environment marked by high macro uncertainty and aggressive valuations.

Key risks for the company and shares include: FX (most of sales are outside of the US), volume headwinds for beer/soda end markets, commodity pressure (generally pass throughs are tight, but occasionally issues pop up), and cyclical risk for the transit packaging unit.

Fanuc (6954-JP)

Upslope first initiated a “Starter” position in Fanuc in April 2025. It has been an undisclosed Starter since and has grown (both organically and inorganically) into a full-sized position. A somewhat belated overview of Upslope’s thesis is below.

Based in Japan, Fanuc is a global leader in industrial automation. The company holds ~50% market share in CNCs (effectively the brains/controllers of automated industrial machinery) and is a leading player in factory robotics. End markets served include (largest to smallest): general industrial/manufacturing, automotive, electronics, and aerospace. Geographically, Fanuc is highly diversified, with ~23% of sales from China (down sharply in recent years), 20% U.S., 18% Europe, and 14% Japan.

Given Fanuc’s dominant, embedded position in industrial automation and robotics, few companies appear better positioned to benefit from the potential move of AI into the “physical” world. Rising trade barriers, which effectively boost global reshoring, should also provide an overall tailwind for Fanuc’s products and services. While shares have rebounded sharply over the last few quarters, valuation is not unreasonable today at ~20x forward EBITDA – particularly considering consensus estimates that assume only modest (LSD%) revenue growth and margin expansion in the years ahead. Like many leading Japanese industrials, the company has a fortress balance sheet, with no debt and cash of ~2.5x EBITDA.

Key risks for the company and shares include: significant exposure to China (uncertain macro and significant competition), highly cyclical product and end market exposure (e.g. autos), FX, and general political/policy uncertainty (given exposure to global trade).

Henry Schein (HSIC)

Henry Schein is the leading distributor of dental products in the U.S. and various countries around the world. The company also distributes more general healthcare products. Within its core Dental business, the company holds the leading market share position in all major countries (52% of Dental sales are from the U.S., 28% EMEA, 20% Canada/RoW) in which it operates – generally holding ~25-40% share. Historically, the company has been a slow, steady and mostly a-cyclical grower.

In January 2025, KKR announced an agreement to take a strategic stake in Henry Schein. Given their now-15% stake (up from an initial 12%), KKR has actively worked with the board and management to enhance shareholder value. In addition to cost controls, the company has also focused on and seen accelerated growth in its smaller, but (much) higher margin segments (Specialty Products, which includes the company's private label offerings, and Technology). At just 15x 2026 consensus earnings (vs. 12-25x historically), with a highly defensive business model, and optionality surrounding KKR's stake and involvement (continue to improve the business or potentially take it private), HSIC shares appear attractive.

Key risks for the company and shares include: uncertainty surrounding KKR's stake, CEO transition (potentially a positive as efficiency efforts accelerate, but still presents uncertainty), consolidating end markets, and broader healthcare policy uncertainty.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan: Huge Breakout - Now What?
data.description:
Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) has broken to new 15-year highs, while copper has broken to new all-time highs. This article looks at what is behind the breakout, why it looks genuine, and what could come next.

The Fog Lifts

When I last wrote about FCX in May, I was generally positive and wanted to buy around $34 for a $50 target. However, the situation was far from clear or bullish, as copper prices were rising ahead of expected tariffs, and no one really knew what tariffs would be announced or what their effect would be. Furthermore, there was still a lot of uncertainty surrounding the trade war and its effect on the global economy. To make matters worse, problems with FCX's Indonesian operations severely impacted sales.

A lot of this uncertainty has now been cleared. 50% tariffs were announced on semi-finished and copper-intensive derivative products, and while they caused a dip in FCX, and a massive 20% one-day plunge in copper prices, the initial shock soon cleared, and both copper and FCX recovered.

The tariffs are actually good news for FCX, as they are focused on processed copper, such as cables and wires. The ores and concentrates FCX imports are exempt. Furthermore, the tariffs have resulted in a premium for US copper prices over LME copper.

Freeport highlighted a $1.25 per pound U.S. premium over LME copper prices, resulting in an "approximate $1.7 billion annual financial benefit on Freeport's U.S. sales."

Markets hate uncertainty, and much of the uncertainty from early 2025 has now cleared. Indeed, the situation is better than many expected.

The Breakout

Some breakouts fail, and this can lead to a nasty reversal. This happened in 2024 when the rally broke $52 but reversed the next week and dropped nearly 50%.

Failed breaks tend to reverse in the first or second week and never manage to show sustained strength above the breakout point.

The current breakout looks different. There was a wobble in the last week of the year when it looked like $52 held as resistance again, but the week starting 5th January made a convincing break. The Monday session gapped higher by over 3% and held its strength, leading to continuation and a gain of around 9% for the week. That has followed through with strong action this week.

But let's not get too carried away. FCX has broken to new 15-year highs, but the all-time high from 2008 is just above at $63.62, and a band of potential resistance starts around $60.

2026 Outlook

The outlook in the years ahead largely hinges on copper and gold prices staying elevated. FCX operations have been severely impacted by the smelter fire in Indonesia in 2024 and the Grasberg incident in 2025. In 2024, FCX sold 4.1 billion pounds of copper and 1.8 million ounces of gold. That is likely to drop to 3.5 billion pounds of copper and 1.05 million ounces of gold in 2025 and even lower in 2026. Even by 2027, the estimates are overall lower than in 2024.

FCX is missing out on the highest prices ever. Yes, revenues are getting a boost, but not by much.

I am disappointed by the lost opportunity. All going well, production could have been over 4 billion pounds and could have even risen closer to 4.5.

Thankfully, the gold rally is showing no signs of slowing, and the criminal charges brought against Fed Chair Powell have boosted prices further as it eroded trust in Fed independence and the US dollar.

Copper is also rallying as demand remains high. Electrification, and particularly new AI data centers, requires large amounts of copper.

That said, the uptrend in copper is a lot slower than that of gold or silver. It's more like two steps forward and one step back.

That suggests FCX may struggle to maintain a break of the $63.62 all-time high. If production dips in 2026 and copper fails to maintain a strong trend, then revenues will be largely flat. However, it's not all about revenues, as FCX initiatives could boost EPS in the coming years.

Earnings Preview

FCX is due to report Q4 earnings on the 22nd of January. Both revenues and EPS are expected to drop due to the Grasberg incident.

FY '25 EPS is expected to come in at 1.57, which would give FCX a rather unattractive PE ratio of 36.9 at current prices. However, earnings are expected to grow strongly in the coming years.

EPS is expected to grow much faster than revenue.

This is due to the lean initiatives in the US leading to higher production and lower costs.

As the last conference call outlined,

And over the next couple of years, as we bring on new leach pounds through these additives that we've been trialing and more importantly, through adding heat to the equation, we're going to be able to scale the leach from 300 million -- roughly 200 million now to 300 million next year and up from there to approach our target within the 2030 time frame of 800 million pounds...

And we're very, very, very excited about the opportunity, the value creation opportunity to scale the leach initiative.

It's good news for the future, but we have no idea where copper prices will be in the coming years, and I don't think it will be a major driver in 2026. Q4 earnings could stall the rally as they will remind investors of the current challenges in Indonesia. I expect some consolidation in the $60-64 area.

Conclusions

The breakout above the 2022 and 2024 highs looks genuine, and $52 should now act as support on any subsequent dips. However, there is still potential resistance above in the $60-63.62 area. While FCX may push above temporarily, I don't expect another sustained breakout to happen in 2026, as the drivers are too mixed. Production will be lower, and the company is missing out on selling over 500 million pounds at the highest prices ever. Furthermore, the copper rally is relatively weak and prone to large dips. I rate FCX a hold.

data.title: VICI Properties: A Bargain In The REIT Market
data.description:
Introduction

VICI Properties (VICI) has been a laggard since I rated it a Buy, back in early July based on the inherent booming economy of Las Vegas. With all that has been happening for the company, such as the golden portfolio deal, I want to assess if the REIT is still in a position to keep its buy rating.

To remind readers, the company is one of the biggest REITs available on the market, specializing in experiential real estate with some of the most famous casinos and hotels in its portfolio, operating under a triple-net business model.

Current Dynamics

VICI has realized one of its most strategic moves since its massive MGM acquisition by acquiring the Golden Portfolio back in November. This marks VICI’s formal entry into the Las Vegas Locals market and a strong expansion into Southern Nevada. This includes the STRAT Hotel, Casino & Tower, which is located on the North side of the Strip and features the iconic observation tower. It will also add Arizona Charlie’s Decatur and Boulder that generally cater to full-time residents, offering a more stable and year-round cash flow. The initial rent is set to be at $87MM per year at a cap rate of 7.5% which is extremely attractive compared to the past 4-5% rates seen in previous deals. Rent is set to increase 2% annually starting at the third year of the lease, providing a built-in hedge against inflation.

I welcome the deal as it is a way to get a more sticky customer base, unlike the Strip, that has been relying on global tourism and seen levels fall over the past couple of months. It will also reduce the company’s reliance on Caesars and MGM, though the company remains heavily focused on the same geographic area and industry. VICI is expecting the deal to be directly accretive to its AFFO, meaning that it should support further dividend increases once it closes in mid-2026.

The company also announced that Clairvest will acquire the operations of MGM Northfield Park, and VICI will enter into a new triple-net lease with the company, continuing VICI’s diversifying efforts in terms of tenants. This will make Clairvest the 14th tenant in the rotation, and it is actually important to see that VICI is able to recycle operators behind its large and unconventional assets, moving away from a near-total reliance on just a few massive gaming giants.

I want to touch on what many readers picked up during my latest analysis, the apparently not-so-booming Las Vegas economy in which VICI is highly concentrated. The city saw a notable summer slump in 2025 as visitation dropped about 6% compared to the record-breaking period of 2024. The reason is simple, Las Vegas is not as cheap as before and has been turning more and more towards a wealthier clientele. This has eroded some of the local US customer base that cannot keep up with the ever-mounting costs of the city. There was also a decrease in international visitors, such as Canadians, due to current soft tensions between the two countries.

This has translated into operators like MGM and Caesars seeing their revenue dip for the year, and they have been running promotions such as No Resort fees in order to offer a more value-centric approach. But there is a silver lining, as tourism experts are projecting a comeback in 2026, with visitation expected to hit 40.1MM, around a million more than in 2025.

Valuation

I believe that right now the valuation is being disconnected from the company’s fundamental performance, as it has hit record revenues and raised its guidance, but it is currently trading at nearly its 52-week lows, which, in my mind, offers an opportunity for value investors. Its Price-to-AFFO is at around the lowest tier of the company’s peers at 11.81, which is generally lower than in its history. On a Price-to-Book measure, the company is nearly trading at par, at 1.07, one of the lowest points in the company’s history as well. This has translated to one of the highest yields for investors right now at 6.36%, and it is the second highest available among the company’s peers, offering a great floor for valuation.

Based on the slew of deals that the company has made, it has also been the one growing the most in terms of revenue, at 20.8% CAGR over the past 3 years. Outside of deals, the company has CPI-linked escalators, meaning that even if the economy remains flat, VICI will see its revenue grow automatically as consumer prices rise. The company is also trying to broaden its experiential exposure with expansion into high-end leisure and wellness with projects like Canyon Ranch & One Beverly Hills. VICI also still owns around 33-34 acres of undeveloped land adjacent to the Strip, and with Las Vegas continuing to expand with new projects like the A’s Stadium, VICI will be able to either develop the land with a partner or sell/lease it back to a new operator, creating a massive hidden value unlock.

VICI also has elite profitability margins, even outshining most of its peers, thanks to the triple net lease strategy, which allows the company to not have to spend anything on its properties and lets the tenant take care of everything. Moreover, the company is easily covering its debt burden with a Net Debt/EBITDA of 4.4 and an EBITDA/Interest Expense of 4.4 as well, which lets us know that they are below their target and are not overleveraged. VICI should have ample dry powder for more deals even in a high-interest-rate environment.

Risks

There is still extreme tenant concentration, mainly Caesars and MGM, that accounts for the vast majority of VICI’s rental income. Moreover, Caesars has seen concerns about its regional gaming leases that could cause financial stress, pushing investors to fear a potential rent reset or lease restructuring even if the Strip assets remain profitable.

Tied to that, the rising costs of living could continue to squeeze the disposable income of VICI’s mass market casino customers, which has already been the case in 2025. The rent coverage could thin out as the profit margin of tenants erodes away, worrying investors and depressing the stock further.

VICI is also highly sensitive to the current interest rate environment, as the company grows by issuing equity and taking on debt to buy new properties. Right now, with the combination of depressed shares and higher interest rates, it has become more expensive for the company to fund new deals, impacting growth down the line.

Conclusion

I believe that right now, VICI Properties is a unique bargain on the market for income-seeking investors. It has one of the best yields in the REIT industry, and with the current risks tied to its concentration, the stock has declined past what is rational, considering all of the positive outlooks and the company beating estimates. I upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy.

Upslope's objective is to deliver attractive, equity-like returns with significantly reduced market risk and low correlation versus traditional equity strategies. Q4 marked a solid ending to a strong year for Upslope – one that saw the Fund deliver strong absolute and relative returns with lower downside volatility.

Market Conditions - DRUCK.AI

While markets are ever-changing, the pace has felt much faster over the last few years. Two related shifts stand out. First, gambling – on sports, geopolitics, and stocks/options – is absolutely everywhere. This is a big and unfortunate cultural shift. Second, AI is also everywhere – particularly on the desktops of buyside analysts and PMs. I suspect this technological shift is part of a not-so-virtuous cycle with the cultural shift towards gambling. A couple years ago legendary investor Stan Druckenmiller noted how he made a quick bet on Argentinian stocks with an assist from AI:

...do you want to hear how I invested in Argentina? It's a funny story...I saw the speech in Davos and it was about 1:00 in the afternoon in my office. I dialed up Perplexity [AI] and I said, give me the five most liquid ADRs in Argentina...It gave me enough of a description that I follow the old Soros rule, invest and then investigate. I bought all of them. We did some work on them. I increased my positions and so far, it's been great.4

In today's market, it certainly feels as if most dollars are being invested exactly like this: aggressive, thematic, and very short-term-focused. While it works for many (and I won't pretend Upslope hasn't participated at times), it also creates more opportunities than ever for nimble, detail-focused investors over both the short- and long-run. One important implication: this evolution has likely helped markets remain more resilient than ever in the face of mounting uncertainty and risks. Whether this is a permanent feature remains to be seen.

Portfolio Positioning

As of this writing, gross and beta-adjusted net exposures were 174% and 40%, respectively. Positioning reflects a high number of perceived opportunities on both (long + short) sides of the portfolio. Gross is elevated above the Fund's typical 150% ceiling due to hedged positions in Alphabet (GOOGL) and Intel.

Key Exits

The Fund exited Chemring (CHG-LON, UK-based niche defense contractor focused on specialty explosives and cyber warfare) due to full valuation and a view that the conflict in Ukraine is increasingly likely to begin winding down. This marks the first time since early 2022 that Upslope is not long any European defense stocks. While the outlook for the sector seems likely to remain bright for years to come, this appears more than adequately reflected in valuations and investor sentiment.

The Fund also exited West Pharmaceutical (WST) (WST, producer of packaging/components for injectable drug delivery devices). This was due to better perceived risk/reward for existing and new components of the Fund's healthcare "basket" (which includes: STERIS, Bio-Rad, and recently added Henry Schein).

New Longs

Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH)

Booz Allen is a consulting firm focused largely on serving the nation's defense (~50% of revenue), intelligence (~15%) and civil (~35%) agencies. The company's work is mostly focused on technology solutions – e.g. digital transformation, cyber defense, and AI deployment. Following the 2024 election of the Trump administration, BAH shares rapidly de-rated from a peak of nearly 30x EPS to a recent trough of 14x – largely due to "DOGE" cost-cutting fears.

While BAH has seen earnings and contract award headwinds under the new administration, Upslope's view is that these will prove temporary and that the stock's current valuation more than compensates investors for short-term challenges and uncertainty. More importantly, Booz should continue to benefit from several accelerating or stable long-term secular tailwinds: rising geopolitical risks, rapidly evolving technology usage and threats, and expanding size of government. While this last point (expanding government) was called into question over the past year, Upslope believes that betting on the government – especially the defense and national security arms – to shrink over the long-run is a very poor bet to make.

Key risks for the company and shares include: government budget and political uncertainty, general technology risks, labor inflation, recent CFO departure, and potential easing of geopolitical tensions.

Crown Holdings (CCK)

Crown is a leading global producer of aluminum beverage cans (80% of sales) and transit packaging/equipment (20%). The company is highly diversified by geography, with 60% of sales generated outside of the United States and 34% from emerging and frontier markets. Upslope has been both long and short (e.g. following a misguided acquisition that management seems to have learned the right lessons from) in the past.

While defensive stocks have been extremely out of favor for some time, Crown has continued to execute well, seeing steady growth and positive earnings revisions. As a result of this and the unfavorable environment for defenses, shares have remained cheap at just 13x earnings and 8x EBITDA (the latter effectively sitting near 10-year lows). At the same time, leverage (2.5x net) and share count (-15% in 5 years) are also at decade lows. This – a cheap, economically defensive stock with healthy secular trends, balance sheet optionality, and an active buyback – seems like a particularly attractive setup for a market environment marked by high macro uncertainty and aggressive valuations.

Key risks for the company and shares include: FX (most of sales are outside of the US), volume headwinds for beer/soda end markets, commodity pressure (generally pass throughs are tight, but occasionally issues pop up), and cyclical risk for the transit packaging unit.

Fanuc (6954-JP)

Upslope first initiated a “Starter” position in Fanuc in April 2025. It has been an undisclosed Starter since and has grown (both organically and inorganically) into a full-sized position. A somewhat belated overview of Upslope’s thesis is below.

Based in Japan, Fanuc is a global leader in industrial automation. The company holds ~50% market share in CNCs (effectively the brains/controllers of automated industrial machinery) and is a leading player in factory robotics. End markets served include (largest to smallest): general industrial/manufacturing, automotive, electronics, and aerospace. Geographically, Fanuc is highly diversified, with ~23% of sales from China (down sharply in recent years), 20% U.S., 18% Europe, and 14% Japan.

Given Fanuc’s dominant, embedded position in industrial automation and robotics, few companies appear better positioned to benefit from the potential move of AI into the “physical” world. Rising trade barriers, which effectively boost global reshoring, should also provide an overall tailwind for Fanuc’s products and services. While shares have rebounded sharply over the last few quarters, valuation is not unreasonable today at ~20x forward EBITDA – particularly considering consensus estimates that assume only modest (LSD%) revenue growth and margin expansion in the years ahead. Like many leading Japanese industrials, the company has a fortress balance sheet, with no debt and cash of ~2.5x EBITDA.

Key risks for the company and shares include: significant exposure to China (uncertain macro and significant competition), highly cyclical product and end market exposure (e.g. autos), FX, and general political/policy uncertainty (given exposure to global trade).

Henry Schein (HSIC)

Henry Schein is the leading distributor of dental products in the U.S. and various countries around the world. The company also distributes more general healthcare products. Within its core Dental business, the company holds the leading market share position in all major countries (52% of Dental sales are from the U.S., 28% EMEA, 20% Canada/RoW) in which it operates – generally holding ~25-40% share. Historically, the company has been a slow, steady and mostly a-cyclical grower.

In January 2025, KKR announced an agreement to take a strategic stake in Henry Schein. Given their now-15% stake (up from an initial 12%), KKR has actively worked with the board and management to enhance shareholder value. In addition to cost controls, the company has also focused on and seen accelerated growth in its smaller, but (much) higher margin segments (Specialty Products, which includes the company's private label offerings, and Technology). At just 15x 2026 consensus earnings (vs. 12-25x historically), with a highly defensive business model, and optionality surrounding KKR's stake and involvement (continue to improve the business or potentially take it private), HSIC shares appear attractive.

Key risks for the company and shares include: uncertainty surrounding KKR's stake, CEO transition (potentially a positive as efficiency efforts accelerate, but still presents uncertainty), consolidating end markets, and broader healthcare policy uncertainty.

Closing Thoughts

In the current environment, it is not difficult to find higher quality businesses trading at attractive valuations. It is very difficult to be confident shares of such businesses will outperform in the near- or even medium-term. This is against a backdrop where the macro and geopolitical risks are extremely high, yet markets remain in full risk-on mode. While timing is always difficult, the picture is quite clear.

Thank you for the trust you've placed in me and Upslope to manage a portion of your hard-earned money. Please contact me if you have any questions at all, would like to add to your investment, or know a qualified investor who may be a good fit for Upslope's unique approach.

Sincerely,

George K. Livadas

Likely overblown fears have allowed for a seemingly attractive entry price in advance of potential share gains and harvesting of recently elevated investment spend.

Japan Exchange Group (8697-JP): Largest exchange operator in Japan with exposure to equities, derivatives, and information services. Key driver and beneficiary of ongoing efforts to reform Japanese equity market structure and corporate governance standards – as well as ongoing global market volatility.

Korea Defense Industry ETF (KDEF): Concentrated ETF with exposure to South Korea's leading defense and shipbuilding contractors. Beneficiary of elevated/rising geopolitical tensions significant cost advantages vs. US/Eur peers, heightened policy uncertainty, and government-backed pro-shareholder/market reforms.

Sandvik (SAND-SE): Sweden-based provider of mining, metal-cutting, and other industrial equipment and tools. Dominant share in mining equipment and outsized exposure to gold miners, combined with modest valuation and elevated gold prices make for an attractive outlook.

Smiths Group (SMIN-LON): Historically sleepy UK-based industrial conglomerate focused on specialty industrial niches and security/detection. Recent activist pressure has led management to commit to transforming the business by shedding non-core units and focusing on the strongest legacy segments.

STERIS (STE): Leader in infection prevention, sterilization, and procedural solutions for healthcare providers, hospitals, pharmaceutical & bioprocess customers, and medical-device makers. Strong historic organic growth, despite majority of sales from service/consumables.

Teledyne Tech. (TDY): Producer of specialty electronics, components, and technologies – primarily focused on imaging sensors and cameras. Dinged by transformative 2021 FLIR acquisition, expectations and valuation have been reset, balance sheet is clean, and the company is positively exposed to reshoring trends and rising defense spending.

Appendix C: Terminology

Core Longs: Higher “quality” businesses (defined as low cyclicality, clean balance sheet, obvious and durable competitive advantages) managed with less valuation sensitivity (i.e. typically won’t exit a Core long solely because of valuation) and assuming a multi-year time horizon.

Tactical Longs: Traditional “value” investments of more modest quality (reasonably or very cyclical, currently experiencing operational, competitive or financial challenges) managed with greater valuation sensitivity (i.e. typically begin to exit if shares approach full valuation) and assuming a shorter time horizon (typically 6-18 months).

Starter Longs: Generally smaller, undisclosed longs that fall into at least one of the following groups:

  1. 1. Ideas where I’ve completed enough work to establish a toehold, but still have more to do.
  2. 2. Companies facing obvious short-term challenges where I believe the near-term path in shares is likely lower, but the stock is ‘cheap enough’ and outright attractive over the longer-term.
  3. 3. ‘Counter-shorts’ – higher-beta longs with good risk/reward that trade more in-sync with Upslope’s shorts on a daily basis and can be used to manage overall net long exposure more effectively and tax-efficiently.

Alpha Shorts: Individual company short positions.

Hedges: Diversified index hedges (either long or short).

data.title: Rates Spark: Positive Impulses, But Treasuries Are Not Moving
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

Wednesday probably sees daylight on the Supreme Court's decision on the IEEPA tariffs. It could rule them legit, and if so, we just move on. We suspect they will be struck down, and we'll probably still just move on. This Treasury market is showing a remarkable capacity to just not care too much about stuff. It won't last, but it's what we've got for now.

The CPI report was net positive for bonds

The biggest number for the bond market on Tuesday was the CPI report. It was tolerably good, in the sense that a 0.2% month-on-month/2.6% year-on-year outcome for core inflation some six months on from the instigation of a large rump of tariffs is a success, as the tariff effect is quite contained in terms of impact on consumer prices.

The impact effect on the 10yr Treasury yield was impressive, as it snapped lower. But it recovered higher subsequently. The resilience of the 10yr yield to various crosswinds has been remarkable of late.

It's baulking at excuses to snap both lower and higher in yield. The auctions this week have been solid too, with 3's 10's and 30's all showing consistent negative tails and decent auction details. But still, minimal follow-through.

The US deficit data so far has also been, if anything, a net positive

The US fiscal deficit for the first quarter of fiscal year 2026 came in at $601 billion, which is actually $110 billion less than the same time last year. A big reason is that tax revenues jumped - up 13%, or $141 billion - thanks to higher income and payroll taxes and a bump in customs duties after tariff changes.

Spending did go up a bit (2%, or $31 billion), but not enough to offset the revenue surge. There’s also a small wrinkle: some payments normally made on 1 January got pushed into December, but even adjusting for that, the deficit would still be about $112 billion lower than last year. Overall, revenues climbed faster than spending, giving the early year deficit a noticeable dip.

The angst with respect to the fiscal deficit has absolutely ramped down in recent months. The spread from 10yr SOFR to 10yr Treasuries is now in the 35bp area, following a steady trek down from the 55bp area seen in September 2025.

That journey has coincided with gradual improvements in the fiscal deficit position, helped by tariff revenue. It's having a meaningful effect, as had the spread remained at 55bp, the 10yr Treasury yield today would be in the area of 4.4% (rather than the sub-4.2% we see currently).

Supreme Court decision should really be impactful, but it probably won't be

Wednesday may well see the Supreme Court decision on the legality of the IEEPA tariffs. Our basic assumption is for them to be struck down, and we think the market is generally of the same opinion.

Confirmation of that would cause some consternation in the Trump administration, but we'd assume that a way will be found to re-erect the tariffs subsequently, either employing current acts of Congress, or writing new acts.

There is no plan B to the tariff plan of President Trump, which he reiterated at Tuesday's speech in Detroit. We're not expecting a huge market reaction as a result, but it will be messy should there be an avalanche of demands for tariff refunds.

data.title: IJS: Choose AVUV Instead For Small-Cap Value Exposure
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Passive small-cap strategies like the one employed by the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS) do not sufficiently screen for quality, thereby placing investor capital at risk when market conditions deteriorate. Instead, the actively managed Avantis US Small Cap Value ETF (AVUV) consistently produces a fundamentally superior portfolio, which is why it has outperformed IJS in every year since its inception. Given this alternative, I rate IJS a "sell."

Fund Overview

According to its fund page, IJT tracks the S&P SmallCap 600 Value Index, which "seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of small-capitalization U.S. equities that exhibit value characteristics." These value characteristics include a stock's book value-to-price ratio, its earnings-to-price ratio, and its sales-to-price ratio. However, the Index also indirectly holds stocks exhibiting weak growth and momentum characteristics, since all S&P SmallCap 600 Index stocks are scored separately on both the value and growth dimensions.

IJS's fund characteristics include a 0.18% expense ratio, a 0.03% 30-day median bid/ask spread, $7.4 billion in net assets, and a 1.53% trailing distribution yield paid quarterly.

IJS Sector Allocations vs. AVUV

As of January 10, 2026, IJS's two largest sectors were Financials (21%) and Consumer Discretionary (17%). However, relative to AVUV, these sectors are 4.5% and 2.10% underweight, respectively. Per the comparison below, IJS's largest overweights are in Real Estate and Health Care. Notably, its exposure to REITs indicates that not all distributions received are qualified.

IJS Performance Analysis

The following log-scaled chart highlights how AVUV's performance lead over IJS has steadily increased since October 2019, the first full month after AVUV's inception. This trend is relevant because it shows that AVUV can outperform in both bull and bear markets, including by 6.43% in 2022 and 8.14% in 2023.

Crucial to AVUV's relative success was its ability to recover from its 16.41% decline from April 2022 to September. As shown below, AVUV recovered by November 2022, whereas IJS experienced both a deeper drawdown (20.65%) and a much longer recovery time. Overall, IJS was underwater by nearly two more years (23 months) during this bear market.

The statistics I compiled from 26 small-cap value ETFs indicate that capital efficiency ratios (ROA, ROE, and ROTC) are closely related to an ETF's recovery time among small-cap funds. Specifically, I found a moderate negative correlation between recovery time and return on total capital for drawdowns ending in September 2022. I also found that Russell 2000 Value Index funds like the iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF (IWN) and the Vanguard Russell 2000 Value Index Fund ETF (VTWV), which have the lowest ROTC figures, have yet to recover as of December 2025. Therefore, it's reasonable to make quality a key metric when evaluating the resiliency of a small-cap value ETF.

IJS Primary Weakness: Poor Quality

IJS's components, as part of the S&P 1500 Composite Index, must meet a positive profitability requirement upon entry. That gives it a slight edge over the Russell 2000 Value ETFs, but overall, IJS's quality is still below average for small-cap value funds. Based on its current composition, IJS ranks #31/40 on ROA, #33/40 on ROE, and #31/40 on ROTC compared to #19/40, #20/40, and #18/40 rankings for AVUV. Therefore, it's evident that AVUV's managers intentionally make capital efficiency a key part of the selection process. I've highlighted these three statistics, plus several margin metrics for IJS's top ten holdings, in the table below.

For small-cap stocks, where margins are generally below those of large-caps, profitability levels are just as important as the direction. This matters because companies operating close to an acceptable margin have increased drawdown risk when market conditions deteriorate. Subsequently, weak capital efficiency ratios limit their ability to reinvest when conditions improve, leading to slower recovery times. This partially explains why IJS performed reasonably well relative to AVUV in 2024 and 2025. During these years, positive market conditions supported lower-margin businesses, but over the full cycle, AVUV weathered uncertainty much better.

IJS Secondary Weakness: Weak Growth and Valuation Mix

AVUV also has a meaningful growth and valuation advantage over IJS. As highlighted below, AVUV's current components increased their earnings per share by an annualized 0.72% over the last three years, while IJS's have declined by 6.04% over the same period. AVUV also has the advantage on three-year sales CAGR (4.26% vs. 1.14%), one-year estimated sales growth (4.09% vs. 3.65%), and one-year estimated EPS growth (5.03% vs. 2.80%).

The table also shows IJS currently trades at 15.57x trailing earnings, which is two multiple points (15%) more than AVUV. Given IJS's inferior quality and growth exposure, this shouldn't happen from a factor investing perspective, and its mix increases the chances of IJS trailing AVUV as markets rotate their preference between each factor.

Risks To Thesis

My thesis could be invalidated if factor discrimination reduces over the next cycle. This scenario would mean that AVUV's higher-quality, higher-growth approach to small-cap value becomes less important, and IJS might benefit more than AVUV because its top ten sub-industry concentration levels indicate greater diversification - 33.8% vs. 43.6%, per the table below.

However, I don't believe this scenario is likely. To illustrate why, consider the following calendar-year returns for various SmallCap 600 Index ETFs covering the value (IJS), growth (IJT), momentum (XSMO), and low-volatility (XSLV) from 2016 to 2025. Cross-factor differences, defined as the variance between the best- and worst-performing factors, averaged 15.82% over this period.

This data indicates that opportunities exist for fund managers aiming to optimize small-cap indices. AVUV does not have a significant advantage over IJS on the value factor, as the two have similar valuation ratios covering sales, cash flow, earnings, and book value. However, its managers have designed a portfolio that's at least competitive on valuation, while significantly enhancing exposure to other key factors, including quality and growth.

Investment Recommendation

With $7.4 billion in assets under management and a 25+ year track record, IJS is a popular choice for investors seeking broad small-cap value exposure. However, it's not an ideal choice. AVUV offers investors similar exposure to the size and value factors while strengthening its overall factor mix, particularly on quality and growth. As a result, it's the superior choice, and I rate IJS a "sell."

data.title: Benign U.S. Inflation Keeps The Door Open For At Least Two Further Rate Cuts
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

Inflation undershoots expectations

The December CPI report has provided another soft inflation print, with headline prices rising 0.3% month-on-month as the consensus forecast, while core inflation rose only 0.2% versus the 0.3% consensus prediction.

We thought there was a strong chance of an even higher print of 0.4%, in large part due to timing issues related to the government shutdown that heightened the influence of price discounting later in November 2025 relative to a 'normal' November 2024. We thought this would have led to a bigger seasonally adjusted bounce for December price levels.

The key takeaway is that goods prices were very benign, which underscores the point that tariffs have had a far more muted impact on inflation than feared.

Goods that are often imported and therefore subject to tariffs include appliances (-4.3% MoM), furniture (-0.4% MoM), new vehicles (0.0% MoM) and video and audio equipment (-0.4% MoM). This is a remarkable story and suggests US retailers are squeezing their profit margins. Used vehicles also fell in price (-1.1%).

Core goods prices show limited tariff impact

Door remains wide open for two interest rate cuts

In terms of sectors that continue to run hotter, the key primary rent and owners' equivalent rent increased 0.3%, medical care services rose 0.4% and transport services increased 0.5%, largely due to a 5.2% jump in airline fares. Apparel prices increased 0.6% and food prices increased 0.7%.

This is a very good outcome overall, and with Fed Chair Jerome Powell suggesting that the tariff impact hasn't got much further to run and could peak in this current quarter, it indicates that policymakers are on course to cut interest rates further.

We still see a bit of risk from tariffs, but the fact it has come through so slowly and much less forcibly than feared gives the opportunity for falling gasoline prices, slowing housing rents and weakening wage growth to contribute to CPI continuing to trend lower this year, getting very close to 2% towards the end of the year. As such, two Fed rate cuts seem perfectly achievable, with the risks skewed towards a third due to the cooling jobs story.

Jobs and affordability will be key for the mid-terms

With regard to the jobs numbers, the ADP private payrolls data suggest employment increased 11,750 per week on average in the four weeks to 20 December. That's consistent with the 50k monthly increase in non-farm payrolls, which is what the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reported last week for December.

Remember, though, that Powell indicated that the Fed believes payrolls growth is being overestimated by around 60,000 per month, so effectively this suggests the labour market is effectively stagnant.

Household affordability concerns are set to be the key issue at the mid-term elections in November. Easing price pressures are a positive development, but it is clear from consumer confidence surveys that households are not necessarily believing it just yet.

We suspect the bigger driver of anxiety is the cooling jobs market, where all sectors outside of government, leisure and hospitality and private education and healthcare services have lost jobs for seven out of the past eight months.

BLS calculations for % of economic output accrued to workers in the form of compensation

On top of this, the chart above shows the BLS’s calculations for the proportion of economic growth that is accrued to workers via their pay, which has dropped from 64% in the 1950s to less than 54% today.

With the corporate sector receiving an increasing share of the benefits of economic growth, this goes a long way to explain why consumer confidence is so weak relative to robust GDP prints and record-high equity markets.

This suggests we could see more efforts from the Administration to generate positive headlines, including the proposed capping of credit card borrowing costs, measures to improve mortgage availability and affordability and removing some items from the scope of tariffs. It also underscores the importance to the President of being able to deliver on the proposed $2,000 tariff dividend ahead of the mid-term elections.
